title: Operating Cadence

content.question: What's your business's weekly/monthly rhythm for reviewing goals and adjusting? Is it actually happening?
sop: Set one fixed weekly or biweekly time to review your key numbers, same day, same time, no exceptions.
Define the 3-5 metrics you'll look at every time, and make sure they're current within the last 7 days.
End every review with at least one decision or adjustment, not just a status update.
Rate Frequency, Data Freshness, and Follow-through 1-10, and rate Waste 1-10 based on how much review time is spent restating old info (see calculation, page 5)
Track whether the adjustments from your reviews are actually getting implemented within a week.
content.explainer: The frequency of the loop determines the speed of learning. No cadence = no compounding.
questions: What's your business's weekly/monthly rhythm for reviewing goals and adjusting? Is it actually happening?
What was the last decision your team made using data that was more than a month old?
Of your last 4 scheduled reviews, how many actually changed a decision versus just confirming what you already believed?
content.equation_example: Rate Frequency, Data Freshness, and Follow-through 1-10 each, then rate Waste 1-10. Frequency 8, Data Freshness 7, Follow-through 6, Waste 3 → OCD = (8×7×6)/3 = 112. Higher OCD means your review rhythm is fast, current, and actually changes decisions; lower means you're meeting often but not learning faster.

caption:
Principle Nº 15: Operating Cadence

Rhythm drives execution. The frequency of the loop determines the speed of learning.

Ask yourself: What's your business's weekly/monthly rhythm for reviewing goals and adjusting? Is it actually happening?
